title: The Absorption Gap: Why Manufacturing Margins Compress When Production Volume Slows
linkedin:
A quiet July on the factory floor doesn't look like a problem. Leave thins the roster, customers ease back in after their own EOFY close, and the margin report still shows the same gross margin percentage it showed in May.

That's exactly the point where the number stops telling the truth.

Most manufacturers cost their products using a standard overhead rate, set once a year against an assumed production volume. The rate doesn't move when actual volume does. A factory running at seventy per cent of the throughput it was costed on is still absorbing the same fixed overhead, spread across fewer units.

Every one of those units is quietly more expensive to make than the cost sheet says it is.

The shortfall doesn't show up in the headline margin figure. It lands in a volume variance account most owners have never opened, sitting well below the number that actually gets discussed at the monthly review. Work in progress and finished goods inventory sit on the balance sheet carrying overhead that was never really recovered, and the gap usually only becomes visible at stocktake or year-end reconciliation.

None of this requires treating a quiet month as a crisis. It requires revisiting the standard cost more often than once a year, and tracking actual units produced against budget weekly rather than discovering the gap months later.

For manufacturers supplying construction, retail or hospitality clients, all of whom slow down in the same window, July is exactly when this pattern shows up.

Full piece on the ProfitPulse blog.

#ProfitabilityAdvice #AustralianBusiness #SMEFinance
facebook:
Quick one for manufacturers: does your July margin report look exactly the same as May's?

It might not be telling you the truth. Most factories cost their products using a standard overhead rate set once a year, based on an assumed production volume. When leave and slow winter orders drop actual output below that assumption, the same fixed overhead gets spread across fewer units, and each one quietly costs more to make.

The margin percentage on the report doesn't move though, because it's built on the standard rate, not what actually happened on the floor. The gap usually only turns up at stocktake.

Do you track actual units produced against budget, or just trust the standard cost sheet?

#Manufacturing
google:
A note for manufacturers on the Queensland, New South Wales and Victorian coast as July settles in. If your factory is a little quieter this month, staff on leave, customers easing back in after their own June 30 close, it is worth knowing that this can affect your true cost per unit even when your margin report looks unchanged.

Most manufacturers cost their products using a standard overhead rate, set once a year against an assumed production volume. That rate does not adjust itself when actual output slows. Fixed costs such as the factory lease, equipment finance and supervisory wages still get spread across the units you make, so a quieter production run means each unit quietly carries more cost than the standard cost sheet shows.

This usually stays invisible until a stocktake or year-end reconciliation forces the numbers back to what actually happened, by which point it has often been building for months.

The simplest fix is tracking actual units produced against your budgeted volume regularly, rather than waiting for the annual cost review. If your business runs a seasonal pattern like this every July, it is worth checking now rather than at year end. Read the full piece on the blog for how the mechanism works and what to track.
